Rout, Chandra Sekhar
coredata.dc:description: Vertical graphene (VG) or vertical graphene arrays have attracted the attention of researchers in recent years, as electrode materials for supercapacitor application due to its unique properties. Although significant progress has been made in growth and supercapacitor application of VG, still many recent developments not yet been reviewed. By attuning the growth of the graphene from horizontal to vertical, its electronic band structure and bandgap can be controlled which is evident from the theoretical and experimental findings. In VG electrolyte ions could smoothly transport through regions of one-dimensional structures and access the electroactive material's surface, and electrons can successfully move in the highly conductive VG to reach the current collector. Furthermore, high surface area can also accelerate other kinetic reactions and the one dimensional structure diminishes strain through volume expansion and contraction. These superiority make VG electrodes captivating in various future energy storage devices including lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors. Herein, the importance of the structure, overview of various plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method of synthesis and the progress in bare and hybrid VG structures are reviewed. Afterward, the important strategies to enhance the energy storage performance by changing the morphology, surface engineering/functionalization and doping of VG are discussed. Furthermore, the challenges and future perspectives for achieving good structural quality with outstanding capacitance performance are listed. This review summarises the importance of vertical graphene structure, PECVD growth and mechanism of VG with recent progress and application towards efficient supercapacitor electrode material.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vertical graphene (VG) or vertical graphene arrays have attracted the attention of researchers in recent years, as electrode materials for supercapacitor application due to its unique properties. Although significant progress has been made in growth and supercapacitor application of VG, still many recent developments not yet been reviewed. By attuning the growth of the graphene from horizontal to vertical, its electronic band structure and bandgap can be controlled which is evident from the theoretical and experimental findings. In VG electrolyte ions could smoothly transport through regions of one-dimensional structures and access the electroactive material's surface, and electrons can successfully move in the highly conductive VG to reach the current collector. Furthermore, high surface area can also accelerate other kinetic reactions and the one dimensional structure diminishes strain through volume expansion and contraction. These superiority make VG electrodes captivating in various future energy storage devices including lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors. Herein, the importance of the structure, overview of various plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method of synthesis and the progress in bare and hybrid VG structures are reviewed. Afterward, the important strategies to enhance the energy storage performance by changing the morphology, surface engineering/functionalization and doping of VG are discussed. Furthermore, the challenges and future perspectives for achieving good structural quality with outstanding capacitance performance are listed. This review summarises the importance of vertical graphene structure, PECVD growth and mechanism of VG with recent progress and application towards efficient supercapacitor electrode material.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical illustration of the importance of growth, structure and morphology of plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposited vertical graphene arrays and their electrochemical energy storage performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever-increasing demand for portable electronics and limited fossil fuels has led the rapid development of energy storage devices like electrochemical supercapacitors and batteries [1–6]. Currently, supercapacitors have attracted the attention of research community for application in electric vehicles, memory backups, railways, automobiles, portable devices and different energy storage systems [7,8]. Compared to batteries that have high energy density, electrochemical capacitors/supercapacitors provide high power density, long cycle life and fast charge-discharge [9,10]. But the limited energy density of supercapacitor constrained its use in some applications. Energy and power density depends on the electrode material's active surface area and electrical conductivity of the electrode. Thus, significant efforts have been paid to enhancing the energy density for practical applications. There are two mechanisms that contribute to the capacitance in supercapacitors: the electric double layer capacitance (EDLC) and pseudocapacitance [11]. The EDLC is due to the charge separation at the electrode-electrolyte interface and the capacitance originates due to the charge transfer between the electrolyte/electrode is called pseudocapacitance. The latter is attributed to reversible redox processes [12]. Graphene a single layer of graphite having sp 2 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. Due to the excellent properties (thin, transparent and flexible) it has widespread applications in different fields such as energy storage devices, field-effect transistors and sensors [13,14]. Further the orientation of the structure like nanowall, nanowire, nanorod and nanotube of carbon and different engineered metal oxide composite structures expedite enhanced supercapacitor performance [15–17]. For graphene the orientation could be either horizontal or vertical, corresponding to graphene in parallel with or perpendicular to the synthesis substrate, respectively.

Vertical graphene (VG) or vertical graphene arrays are a 3-dimensional porous network of inter-connected graphene nanosheets oriented perpendicularly to the substrate. VG is also reported as vertical graphene nanosheets, carbon/graphene nanowalls, nanosheets, nanoflakes and nanoflowers [18–21]. The sheets are self-supported wall structures with lateral and vertical dimensions of 0.1 to tens of micrometers and a thickness of few nanometers. Each sheet consists of 1–10 numbers of graphene layers and of 0.34–0.37 nm inter-layer spacing [22–25]. The unique structure of VG (schematic shown in Fig. 1(a)) possesses amazing properties like vertical orientation, ultra thin sharp edges, open channels and non-agglomerated structure [26]. These unique properties make VGN stable and attractive for many applications like electrochemical capacitors, batteries, fuel cells, solar cells, field emission, gas, bio and chemical sensors, ultrafast terahertz response photoelectric device, ac line filtering, hydrogen absorption, black body coating, spintronics, hydrophobic coatings, photo-catalytic activity, template for other material growth, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy and memory devices [27–41]. The schematic in Fig. 1(b) illustrates various applications of the VG.

Plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition is emerged as an important method for the synthesis of carbon materials like graphene, carbon nanotube, diamonds and vertical graphene [42,43]. Further, in PECVD the dissociation of carbon happens at relatively low temperature than thermal CVD, make this method promising for controlled graphene structure. PECVD offers the advantages of lower substrate temperature, higher growth rate, and better control in nanostructure. Therefore, various plasma CVD are used for the synthesis of VG for controlling its structure and properties. Recently, various experimental studies demonstrated the mass production of VG nanostructures using roll-to-roll PECVD and microwave plasma method for numerous promising energy and environmental applications [44,45]. There are several factors affect the VG structures, like substrate, substrate temperature, plasma power and gas composition for quality VG structure for different applications. Nowadays, binder free and self-supported structure with unique properties like VG have great importance for supercapacitor applications. The importance of vertical orientation in energy storage is also reported in case of some nanowall structured metal/metal oxide and their composites. The pristine and hybrid VG structures are developed in the recent time towards energy storage applications. The hybridization process can improve the electrochemical performance of VG sheets by integrating pseudocapacitive electrode material (such as transition metal oxides, chalcogenides and polyaniline (PANI) with VG sheets. Generally, the pseudocapacitive electrodes possess faradic redox reactions for charge storage and they have higher capacitance but lower power density and weaker cyclic stability than EDLCs. On the other hand, vertically oriented graphene sheets have high surface area, outstanding mechanical strength, and high conductivity; therefore, they can be used as a scaffold for loading pseudocapacitive nanomaterials with high mass loading. Generally, VG acts as assembled nanostructured arrays in vertical orientation on conductive substrates and its rational design with metal/metal oxides can enhance the electrochemical performance [46,47].

Herein, the importance of the VG structure, overview of various PECVD method of synthesis and the recent progress in bare and hybrid VG structures are reviewed. Afterward, the important strategies to enhance the energy storage performance by changing the morphology, surface engineering/functionalization and doping of VG are discussed. Furthermore, the challenges and future perspectives for achieving good structural quality with outstanding capacitance performance are listed. This review summarises the importance of vertical graphene structure, PECVD growth and mechanism of VG with recent progress and application towards efficient supercapacitor electrode material. A schematic representation of the content of the review is given in Fig. 1(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: A general overview of VG's different applications
ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional graphene has many applications in the field of electronic devices, mechanical devices, transparent conducting films, chemical sensors and spintronics devices, due to its interesting properties like excellent electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, high mechanical strength, transparent and high surface area [48,49]. Further the orientation and arrangement of the graphene sheets will enhance the performance and give additional application to the material. Here an overview of the different applications of VG is discussed. VG has sharp, dense and vertically orientated edges with outstanding electrical conductivity and improved contact resistance between substrate and emitter are favorable to the field emission (FE) performance. Also, the edge thickness, height, density and orientation of the VG determine the FE performance. Recently many articles modified the graphene edges by means of changing the synthesis process and technique for enhanced FE performance [31,50]. Zhao et al. reported that nitrogen doping could significantly increase the π density states near the Fermi level that helps upward shift of work function, which resulted in a lower turn-on field [50]. Similarly, the large surface of VG can support functional materials such as metal/metal oxide nanoparticles and metal compounds. These functional materials work as catalysts and used for the applications in bio and chemical sensors, hydrophobic/hydrophilic coatings, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy, hydrogen evolution, and energy conversions. A uniform distribution of platinum nanoparticles on VG shows the hydrophilicity and plasma treatment can also enhance the hydrophilicity of VG [51–54]. Shang et al. demonstrated an ultralow platinum loading of 4.5 μg cm−2 in the application of methanol fuel cells [55]. Zhang et al. reported VG/platinum nanoparticles electrodes which significantly enhanced electro-catalytic performance and cycling stability for direct methanol fuel cells and the hydrogen evolution compared to commercial carbon/platinum electrodes [56]. Surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy was observed in gold nanoparticle deposited VG because of the localized surface plasmon resonance induced by Au nanoparticles [41,57]. Cui et al. reported a gas sensor based on the VG and silver nanoparticle composite and further multi-metal deposition was also carried out, Ni and Cu deposited VG achieved a sensitivity of two orders more than that of other published nonenzymatic glucose sensors in low concentration ranges [58,59]. Bo et al. reported a VG/SnO2 sensor for formaldehyde sensing with high sensitivity [60]. Also, the edges and defects of VG result in high electrocatalytic activity towards several biological applications. An oxygen plasma treatment enhanced the amount of enzyme carried on the edges of VG by creating more functional sites and enhancing the hydrophilicity of VG [61]. Further the VG has also several applications towards flexible/stretchable electrodes due to its good electrical conductivity. Wu et al. reported strain sensors consisting of VG sandwiched by two poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) substrates, these sensors are insensitive to temperature changes and anticorrosive to human sweat [62]. Yang reported a stretchable electronic skin shows highly sensitive response to joint movement, eye movement, and vocal cord vibration, with maximum stretchability of 100 % [63]. A temperature sensor based on VG was also reported by Yang et al. where, the thermal expansion of PDMS induces changes of resistance of VG embedded in it, thus the temperature can be measured by monitoring the resistance [64]. The other applications of VG reported are solar cell, ultrafast terahertz response photoelectric device, ac line filtering, hydrogen absorption, black body coating, spintronics and photo-catalytic activity [30,34,35,37,38,40]. Recently the VG are explored in the application towards energy storage that are discussed in this review article.

Supercapacitors are stored energy, based on charging and discharging processes at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Three types of capacitive nature can describe the charge storage mechanism of supercapacitors (i) electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), (ii) pseudocapacitors and (iii) hybrid supercapacitors [65,66].

EDLCs are the most common and widely studied form of supercapacitors where the capacitance is generated due to the adsorption of both anions and cations at the electrode/electrolyte interface without any faradaic reactions. Fig. 2(a) shows the energy storage mechanism of EDLCs. In EDLCs, during the charging process, electrons moving from the negative electrode to the positive electrode through an external loop in which cations in the electrolyte move towards the negative electrode and anions towards the positive electrode while in discharging process, the opposite phenomenon occurred. The Helmholtz model is the simplest model to express the capacitance of EDLCs which follow the parallel plate capacitor; [67,68]
(1)
C = εA / d
where ‘C’ is the capacitance of EDLCs, ‘ε’ is the permittivity of the dielectric separating the charges, ‘A’ is the surface area of the electrode, and ‘d’ is the effective thickness of the electric double layer (EDL). Typically, carbonaceous materials ranging from activated carbon to graphene have been widely used as electrode materials for EDLCs [69,70]. The electrochemical performance EDLCs is based on three factors: specific surface area, pore size distribution, and an appropriate electrolyte solution. The electrochemical profile of EDLCs is shown in Fig. 2(b, c), where the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve reveals the rectangular in nature, and the charge-discharge (CD) curve shows a symmetric triangular behavior [71,72].

In spite of the exceptional development in properties of EDLCs, their limited energy generally restricts EDLCs-based supercapacitors to applications in various electronics appliances. Therefore, many research efforts are concentrated on the synthesis of high-performance electroactive materials to overcome the challenge of the low energy of supercapacitors. Supercapacitors with higher energy densities can be obtained using pseudocapacitive materials that store energy by the faradaic process that involves surface or near-surface fast reversible redox reactions [73,74]. The charge storage mechanism in pseudocapacitors can be divided into two types (i) surface redox and (ii) intercalation pseudocapacitors [75]. Fig. 2(d–f) shows the energy storage mechanism, CV and CD profiles for surface redox pseudocapacitors, where charge storage is due to the charge transfers (or redox reactions) occurring at the surface of the electrode. The CV and CD curves closely resemble the EDLCs based supercapacitors, indicating the charge storage's linear dependency over the whole potential window. Various electroactive materials such as RuO2, MnO2 and Fe3O4 exhibit intrinsic (redox) pseudocapacitance. They can store better energy compared to the EDLCs due to fast and reversible redox reactions at the electrode surfaces [76,77]. Aside from surface redox reactions, some layered materials such as Nb2O5, TiO2(B), and MoO3 undergo faradaic charge transfer due to the intercalation of electrolyte ions into the tunnels, this type of pseudocapacitors known as intercalation pseudocapacitors. Fig. 2(g–i) represents the charge storage mechanism of intercalation pseudocapacitors [8,78]. Similarly, the battery-type electrode materials also store charge through faradic reactions, but these materials have chances to change their crystallographic phases during electrochemical reactions, but in the case of pseudocapacitive electrodes, this can't occur. The charge storage mechanism of battery-type electrodes is provided in Fig. 2(j–l), which indicates a couple of well-defined redox peaks in CV profiles and flat (plateau) CD profiles [79,80].

A hybrid supercapacitor can be achieved by hybridization at either the device architecture or the level of the materials. The main aim of hybrid supercapacitors is to obtain an energy density of the battery-like electrode and power density of the capacitor-like electrode in the same device with good cycle life. A hybrid supercapacitor involves one battery-like charge storage process and one capacitive (either EDLC-type or pseudocapacitive) charge storage process, as shown in Fig. 3
[81–83].

The high surface area with other excellent features like high electrical conductivity, chemical stability makes VG a good candidate for the EDL electrode material in supercapacitor applications. Most of the surface area of VG is directly accessible for the charge accumulation and transport and the phase angle of the VG EDLC reached −45° at 15000 Hz in comparison with 0.15 Hz for the activated carbon EDLC, which indicates a much better high-frequency performance of the VG in EDLC [35]. Zhang et al. studied the effect of morphology and orientation of vertical graphene on the performance of supercapacitor is examined using a combination of model calculation and experimental study. Simulation results demonstrate that the orientation and morphology of graphene sheets strongly impact the ion transport capability and the charge storage performance. The areal capacitances of a vertical graphene electrode reach 8.4 mF cm−2 at 100 mV s−1 which is about 38 % higher than that of a lateral graphene electrode. The simulation model used the continuum model of Nernst-Planck, which describe flux of ions under the influence of both the ionic concentration gradient and the electric field. Also, the current density distribution is calculated by Butler-Volmer equation, which shows that the current density is much larger along the top edge of the vertical graphene sheet than the planar surface, and has a maximum value at the corners of graphene sheets [84]. Further by modifying the intrinsic hydrophobic VG to hydrophilic and increasing the VG heights the electroactive surface area is enhanced that leads to further an increase in capacitance [52,53,84,85]. Yang et al. synthesize VG with diverse heights and edge densities and studied that the edges play a predominant role on the charge storage behavior of VG. The charge distribution of a VG plane is obtained with density functional theory calculations and molecular dynamics simulation system to gain the insights into the microscope EDLC structures. Compared with the basal planes, the edges present higher initial charge density (by 4.2 times), higher ion packing density (by 2.6 times), closer ion packing location (by 0.8 Å), and larger ion separation degree (by 14 %) due to the quasi-localized p z state of the edge sites [86]. Further, the molecular dynamics simulation results suggest that the edges advanced the kinetics of electrolyte movement into the interior of VG, resulting in an improved electrolyte accessibility [86,87]. Further to improve the capacitance, pseudocapacitive materials are decorated on VG or composite structures are prepared, which are discussed in the later part of the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantageous features of VG for supercapacitor applications
ce:section.ce:para: Vertical graphene has many exciting supercapacitor electrode characteristics that are useful for the capacitor performance improvement. The different factors help VG as a suitable supercapacitor electrode are discussed and shown in schematic Fig. 4 .

The capacitance of EDLC is attributed to the active surface area. Active surface area plays an important role in electrode-electrolyte interaction. Carbon based materials like activated carbons (ACs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene are proposed for the EDLC due to their high active surface area [68,88]. The ACs and horizontal graphene structures need binders for electrode fabrication leads to a reduction of surface area. VG is directly can be grown on substate and there is no need of binder for electrode fabrication. The high surface area (~1100 m2 g-1) and non-agglomerated structure of VG gives easy access for ion adsorption and diffusion for enhanced capacitance [26,35]. Further, the surface of VG generally hydrophobic in nature and can be modified to hydrophilic by easy plasma-treatment, which in turn enhances the active surface area for ion adsorption [52,53]. VG also contains dense edge planes which enhances charge storage performance as the edge planes provide higher capacitance (50–70 μF) than the basal planes [35].

The electrodes prepared from ACs suffer extra electrolyte resistance due to limiting movement of ions inside the pores of the AC. The extra electrolyte resistance comes from the distributed charge storage at the porous structure of ACs. The vertical orientation and open networked structure are exposed and directly accessible for electrolyte without the charge distribution at the surface, this facilitates easy ion migration and reduces the ionic resistance.

VG contains a continuous carbon layer at the base of the structure that helps in higher in-plane electrical conductivity than that of out of plane [26]. This helps in faster charge transport in the electrode.

VG is a self-supported structure and without use of any binder that reduces the contact resistance between the active material and current collector. The continuous carbon layer is act like current collector.

Pore size plays a measure role in capacitor capability and frequency response. When the pore size is close to the ion size, maximum capacitance is possible and both smaller and larger pore size leads to drop in capacitance. In case of mostly used active material ACs, the poor size is small, which leads to a drop in capacitive behavior at high frequency [35]. The vertical orientation and open structure of VG reduces the porosity effect at high frequency. Thus, 120 Hz ac line filtering is possible in case of VG electrode, which has application in 120 Hz ac to dc for line-powered electronics [35]. Further, kHz ultrafast supercapacitor is reported by Ren et al., where VG is grown on Ni foam. The grown VG exhibit capacitance of 0.32 mF cm−2 at 1 kHz [89].

Recently pseudocapacitive materials (charge storage by faradic reactions) like RuO2, MnO2, NiO, conducting polymers and different (oxygen & nitrogen) surface functional groups are commonly used for enhanced capacitance [52,90]. These materials give poor cycle stability compared to EDLC. Combining these materials with VG, high performance capacitor can be achieved. The self-supported and open-networked structure of VG act like a template material for pseudocapacitive material growth [18,91,92]. Further, the structure helps in high mass loading and improving adhesion with pseudo species. Some VG based pseudocapacitors RuO2/VG [93], MnO2/VG [94,95], polyaniline/VG [96], NiO/VG [97,98] and functionalized VG [52,53] are reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different PECVD and parameters for control growth
ce:section.ce:para: Commonly, VG growth is synthesized by different PECVD systems and different parameters (substrate, substrate temperature, precursor, precursor composition, plasma power) significantly affects the structure of the VG. Microwave plasma CVD [23,99–102], radio frequency plasma CVD [43,103–106] with inductive or capacitive coupled plasma are mostly used for the synthesis. Apart from these PECVD, other plasma systems are also used for VG synthesis and that are discussed in this section. The important advantages of PECVD are low temperature and rapid synthesis as compared to other methods like chemical and physical synthesis techniques. These advantages arise from the unique properties of plasma and plasma-surface interactions which make possible by ionization, excitation and dissociation of the precursor gases. Then by means of controlling the plasma confinement, gaseous precursors, plasma energy and delivery of ion energy to the substrate surface, 3D materials like VG are possible with high control of morphology, structural uniformity and quality [107]. PECVD allow the control over energy and supply of material which ensures bulk synthesis of the nanostructures. One more important feature is that the PECVD produce specific carbon allotropes during the growth [108]. In addition, the presence of strong electric field in the plasma can direct the growth of 2D and 3D material in a vertical orientation from the substrate, leading to the synthesis of highly crystalline and vertically oriented structures [109]. Further, by PECVD technique variety of substrate according to the required applications can be used for the growth of VG nanostructures. During the PECVD synthesis process the VG surface termination can be easily change with in-situ plasma treatment which makes the surface polar that in turn improve the surface wetting property, which is helpful for the supercapacitor applications. Here, we have discussed the different plasma sources and effect of different parameters on the growth of VG.

After the discovery of the VG during the synthesis of CNTs by arc discharge evaporation of graphite, many research has been carried out for the synthesis by other plasma sources, as plasma-based CVD gives high level of controllability, quality of the nanomaterials. Plasma sources with different power frequencies such as microwave (MW) plasma (2.45 GHz), radio frequency (RF) plasma (13.56 MHz), dc plasma and their combinations, have been developed for VG synthesis. The different plasma systems are discussed here and tabulated in Table 1 .

The microwave plasma reactors used for VG synthesis operate at characteristic microwave frequency such as 2.45 GHz. The schematic of the MW-PECVD setup is shown in Fig. 5(a). The MW-PECVD is composed of a microwave generator system, an electromagnetic coil, reactor chamber and gas dispense systems. The reactor chamber is connected to the microwave generator by a rectangular waveguide, which helps to travel the microwave. There are two typical wave propagation modes, i.e., transverse electric (TE) mode and transverse magnetic (TM) mode, referred to as the electric field and magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of wave, respectively. TM mode microwave is used for high-quality VG synthesis to overcome the disadvantage in case of the TE mode, where the high microwave power and direct coupling with the chamber decreases the growth efficiency and uniformity. For TM-mode, a common converter is used to an axisymmetric mode configuration from a TE10 rectangular to a TM01 circular mode [42]. Microwave power is introduced to the plasma chamber through a copper antenna and a window made of quartz. The forward power and reflected power were measured using a directional coupler (Fig. 5(a)). The microwave sources are protected from the reflected power using an isolator with a matched load. Fine-tuning of the microwave circuitry was obtained using a three-stub tuner. A magnet coil is arranged around the periphery of the cylindrical quartz for electron cyclotron resonance plasma excitation. For surface wave microwave (SW-MW) PECVD a coaxial-type open-ended dielectric cavity is used as plasma source. Due to the size of the aperture, an electric field of the microwave exponentially decreases from the disk surface towards the substrate. Therefore, this plasma source can produce high-density planer plasma [115]. A substrate holder, equipped the substrate can be applied a positive/negative dc bias voltage. Different sizes of plasma chamber are also used in the literature [42,115] for large area growth.

RF frequency plasma is another common plasma source used for VG synthesis. Generally, two types of mode are used to couple the energy from the RF generator to generate plasma i.e., i) inductive coupled plasma (ICP) and ii) capacitive coupled plasma (CCP). ICP case the RF power is coupled with inductive circuit (helical or spiral) near to the plasma formation region (Fig. 5(b)), whereas capacitive coupled plasma (CCP) can be produced by a pair of parallel plane electrodes separated by a small distance, with one electrode connected to the power supply while the other is grounded (Fig. 5(c)). The experimental system of RF with CCP consists of (i) a quartz tube reactor chamber with suitable arrangement for vacuum pump, gauges, and gas/monomer feed, (ii) a heater wounded onto the quartz tube reactor chamber and controlled by a variable voltage controller, and (iii) 13.56 MHz RF generator connected to capacitively coupled ring electrodes across the heated reactor zone. Alternative plasma sources have been used for VG synthesis in combination with other high-density plasma sources, such as CCP + ICP [111], and ICP + MW [101]. This combination design takes advantage of the simultaneous formation of high carbon radicals for large area synthesis and the sufficient production of H radicals for the removal of excess amorphous carbon [111].

Vizinearu et al., used an expanding RF plasma system combines magnetron sputtering for catalyst deposition and PECVD for carbon growth in a single reactor (Fig. 5(d)). The analysis focuses on the correlation between the material properties and the plasma characteristics measured at different points along the flow axis, aiming to reveal the importance of plasma species in the growth process [112]. Other plasma like cathodic vacuum arc deposition shown in Fig. 5(e) [113,125], DC plasma/thermal jet plasma [23,114,126–130], hot filament/hot wire plasma [131–134], radical injection CVD [135,136] has been also used for the synthesis of VG structure. In case of radical injection PECVD (RI-PECVD) a parallel-plate with very high frequency (100 MHz) capacitively coupled plasma region and a surface-wave microwave excited H2 plasma as a radical source is installed. High frequency power was introduced to the upper electrode, while the lower electrode was grounded [135].

In case of the direct plasma synthesis, the plasma reactor consists of a quartz tube that houses a tungsten needle cathode, a grounded graphite rod anode act as substrate holder, and a dc high negative voltage supply (up to −10 kV) to drive the dc glow discharge [23]. There are two type of DC plasma system used one is parallel plate type (Fig. 5(f)) and the other one is pin to plate type (Fig. 5(g)). Also, the plasma source to substrate distance and the plasma source power affects the structure of VG. Ghosh et al. [137], reported the detailed change in structure with plasma source to substrate distance and the plasma source power. The number density of vertical sheets decreases with the distance, while the dimensions of the sheets and intersheet spacing of VG increases. By varying the distance and plasma source power, the plasma density changes near the substrate changes, which greatly affects the VG structure shown in Fig. 6 .

There are also other ways to grow VG but PECVD technique provides high growth rate and great control of the VG structure. So, there are about 90 % literatures on VG where the growth has been carried out by PECVD technique. The other nonplasma techniques used for the VG synthesis are magnetron sputtering [21,138], chemical/spray pyrolysis [139], thermal CVD [140] and carbonization. Shi et al. propose a facile method to fabricate VG by directly pyrolyzing copper formate powder at high temperature, where a composite of VG and copper was formed. After sintering the composite showed a tensile strength of 498 MPa. Further by introducing H2 during the pyrolysis process resulted an increase in conductivity of the composite [141]. Recently Wang et al. directly converted waste tires into 3D crosslinked graphene through alkaline-assisted pyrolysis process. The waste tire was firstly converted into powder, and then it was mixed with KOH and then heated under different temperatures (800 to 1000 °C) for different times (2 to 8 h) to obtain the black powder which was thoroughly washed with 0.3 M HCl solution to remove the impurities [142]. VG are prepared by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis of ethanol and fullerene mixture, where a large quartz tube of 100 cm used and the VG growth was observed on the substrates placed at downstream of the tube below at 100 °C [139]. Carbonization of organic precursor and wastes was also used to synthesize VG structure. Li et al. carbonized polyacrylonitrile (PAN) foam in presence of ammonia at 900 to 1200 °C to obtain the VG structure. The formation of the structure was attributed to the preferable etching of ammonia on the flat (002) planes than the vertical ones and the obtained random oriented VG structure is due to the disordered PAN molecular chain [143].

The precursor gas composition and percentage significantly affect the plasma properties and VGs morphology and structure. The different gas composition used in literature for the synthesis of VG is tabulated in Table 1. Hydrocarbons (CH4 and C2H2) are the commonly used hydrocarbons for the VG synthesis [43,99–101,103,110,122]. In addition to that CO2 and CO also used for the carbon source in some literature, Hung et al. [117], introduced a mixture of CO2 and CH4 as reactants at a specified ratio of 2:3 for the VG synthesis. Mori et al. [123], performed VG growth in a microwave plasma system with a CO/H2 precursor. The carbon sources produce reactive carbon dimers (C2) that plays a crucial role in the VG synthesis. These dimers help in the evolution of the VG sheets by insertion into the nucleation sites, which is reported by many groups [25,123,144,145]. Teii et al. [144], studied that C2H2 produce the C2 dimers by direct dissociation of the strong CC bond whereas CH4 easily converts CHx (x = 1–3) radicals to produce the C2 dimers by recombination of the radicals. For removal of the amorphous carbon and unwanted byproducts, carbon precursor is used with other etchant gas like H2, N2 and NH3, which also affects the morphology [110,115,139].

Suzuki et al. [146], studied the effect of hydrogen to carbon ratio on the structure and morphology of VG grown on Si and SiO2. At low H2/CH4 flow rate ratios (0–2), the wall size increased with increasing H2/CH4 flow rate ratio, however at high H2/CH4 flow rate ratios (2–4), the wall size decreased as shown in Fig. 7(a–b). This indicates that hydrogen plays roles, which affects the nucleation due to high etching at high ratios, leads to a change in the morphology (Fig. 7(c)). Ghosh et al., studied the effect of nitrogen on the structure of VG [119]. They found that N2 along with carrier gas Ar, enhanced the VG growth rate (Fig. 7(d)), structural quality and electronic properties. So, the discussion illustrates that the precursor, carrier and etchant gas and their composition or proportion controls the VG morphology and structure.

In case of PECVD synthesis the thermal energy is introduced by thermal heating the substate or plasma heating. VG morphology and structure depends on the substrate nature and its temperature. The nature of the VG structure i.e., the vertical orientation is retained with the use of different substrate but the density and size changes. Because, from the proposed growth model it is clear that a buffer layer is formed for any type of substrate, from where the nucleation starts. For different substrate the surface roughness, surface energy and thermal conductivity affects the nucleation density, leads to a change in VG morphology and growth rate. Different substrates Si [99,102,110,115,123,124], SiO2 [42,43,103,119], Ni [121,147], Cu [100,101,120–122], carbon cloth [117], quartz [83,147], Ti [148], Glass [42,102,116], Al [149], and Au [150] are mostly used for the VG synthesis. Ghosh et al., reported the role of substrate on nucleation and growth of VG grown by MW-PECVD. The VG are grown on Pt, Ni, Au, Cu, Si (100), Si (111), SiO2 and quartz substrates simultaneously, where the morphology of VG varied significantly with substrate [147]. For different applications VG synthesis is reported on the pattern, cylindrical and CNT substrate.

High energy plasma can itch the surface, so the growth of VG in low temperature plasma is used for the VG synthesis. Further low temperature plasma could lead to lower electron density and growth rate. Another factor, substate temperature plays an important role on the VG structure, as it strongly affects the surface reaction kinetics. Generally, the substrate temperature 500–650 °C has been used for successful VG growth in PECVD systems. Rao et al. [151], reported the growth of VG nanosheets at low substrate temperature of 400 °C, however that leads to the formation of some spherical nanoparticle. Ghosh et al. [137], studied the evolution of the VG structure with substrate temperature and found the that substrate temperature from 600 to 800 °C leads to a transformation of the structures from continuous nanographitic layers to interconnected network of vertical network. At 625 °C the VG growth starts and further increase in temperature led to a fully grown VG structure. Which concludes that with increase in substrate temperature the nucleation sites increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth mechanism of VG grown by PECVD
ce:section.ce:para: Although the growth of VG can be done by some other methods/techniques than PECVD, the growth mechanism of VG during the PECVD process mostly has been discussed in recent years. There are many parameters like substrate, growth temperature, precursor, plasma power and plasma source can affect the final VG structure. These parameters, different PECVD synthesis technique and complex nature of plasma makes very challenging to explain the VG growth mechanism. Theory like the vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) or vapor-solid-solid (VSS) explained the growth of structures on catalyst, cannot be used to understand the PECVD growth of VG which do not require catalyst. Several groups studied the mechanism of VG growth and have made a clear picture of the growth [39,147,152,153]. The growth generally based on steps like i) nucleation, ii) growth, and iii) termination. The irregular cracks and dangling bonds on the substrate act as the nucleation sites that helps to form buffer layer for the VG growth. Then due to the internal stress, localized electric field and anisotropic growth, the graphene layers start to grow in vertical direction and carbon atoms are continuously fed into the open edges of the structure. The growth stops upon the closure of the open edges, which is determine by the balance between deposition of the material and etching rate by plasma [153].

The nucleation is initiated by the adsorption of hydrocarbon radicals on the substrate that forms a very thin buffer layer of amorphous carbon/carbide due the mismatch in the lattice parameters of carbon species and substrate (Fig. 8(a–c)). Once the buffer layer is formed then the VG nanosheets start to grow on the buffer layer, this explains why the similar morphology VG grown on different types of substrates. The defects or dangling bonds in the buffer layer act as the nucleation sites, which promotes the growth of the graphene onions/nano graphitic petals in the vertical direction confirmed from the HRTEM micrographs [39,152]. The graphene onions act like the nucleation site for the VG growth, then the carbon species attach to the graphene onions are easily bonded and then the growth expand in the direction of radical diffusion, perpendicular to the electrode plane. The thickness of the VG varies from 5 to 10 layers of graphene that depends on the number of layers that nucleates at the bottom.

Based on the literature and theoretical investigations, the vertical growth is likely due to three main reasons i.e., the electric field, the internal stress, and the anisotropic growth effects. The electric field in the plasma plays role in the orientation of the carbon nanostructures. An aligned parallel array of carbon nanosheet in the direction of local electric field is observed at the surface of a conducting surface [153]. The effect of the electric field on VG growth is found that modulating the local electric field above the substrate can effectively control the density and orientation of the VG networks (Fig. 8(d)) [150]. The electric field induces anisotropic dipoles in the graphene nanosheets that helps in the vertical oriented structure. Recently, Sun et al., reported that the VG growth occurs at the strong electric site, VG coating on the GaN nanowires has dominant effect of the local electric field, making the VG denser and longer in the tip region of the nanowires than on its body [154]. The numerical simulation study of growth kinetics of the VG confirmed that the uniform growth at high density plasma due to the regular electric field in the vicinity of the plasma grown nanostructures [155].

Internal stress arising from the temperature gradients, ion bombardment, lattice mismatch between the substrate material and the graphitic material cause defects in the buffer layer, which work as the nucleation sites for the VG growth. The growth of onion-like graphitic layers eventually switches to upward growth of graphene sheets. The dissociated carbon species in the plasma will continuously provide radicals, ions, and neutrals to the open sites of the hexagonal lattices of VG sheets.

The anisotropic growth of the carbon species also affects the directional growth of VG. It has been reported that the growth rates in the parallel and perpendicular directions to the graphene layer were different [156]. During the growth the carbon species diffusing towards the substrate instead of towards the growing edges is re-evaporated due of the weak van der Waals force with substrate. In addition, due to the sharp features of the VG edge with strong localized electric fields, carbon atoms also preferentially grow from the edges of VG (Fig. 8(e)). Although several studied reports the growth mechanism of VG from gaseous precursor by PECVD, still the growth of VG from liquid or solid precursors is much less explored. Still many open questions are there like, the mechanism of secondary nanosheet growth and variation in density of the VG. Therefore, for better quality and controlled VG, more studies are necessary in this direction.

There are some recent literatures synthesized the vertical graphene on some different substrate like alumina membrane, GaN and titanium. The nanoporous alumina is widely used for drug delivery, catalysis, energy storage, sensors and various biological applications. Yerlanuly et al. [148], reported the synthesis of VG on nanoporous alumina membrane synthesized by RI-PECVD method. Where the pore sizes and thicknesses of the nanoporous membrane affect the height, morphology, and structural characteristics of VG. In case of alumina membrane of pore size 200, it is found that the VG replicate the surface structure of nanoporous membrane (Fig. 9(a, b)). Sun et al. [154], grown the VG by PECVD on GaN nanowire and demonstrated the growth preferably occurs at spots where the local field is stronger i.e., at GaN nanowire tips (Fig. 9(c, d)). On the round shaped nanoparticles, the VG grows along field direction, instead of being perpendicular to the substrate. Further, the theoretical calculation points out that when the VG grows, the electric field can accumulate electrons on the flakes, which eventually turns the growth mode from 2D into 3D by virtue of the Coulombic repulsion. VG on titanium substrate is deposited by PECVD by Zhang et al. [157], they studied the photothermal property of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 immersed in 1 mL of ultrapure water are exposed to the 808 nm laser at a power of 0.8 W cm−2 for 10 min. After near-infrared (NIR) irradiation, surface temperatures of Ti, VG@Ti-650, and VG@Ti-750 increased to 46.1 °C, 51.7 °C and 57.4 °C respectively (Fig. 9(e)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supercapacitor applications of vertical graphene arrays

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a promising electrode material for supercapacitor applications due to its high surface area for charge accumulation, high electrical conductivity, and outstanding thermal and structural stabilities. However, the traditional graphene sheets are stacked horizontally on a substrate due to the strong van der Waals interaction, which hampers the accessibility of electrolyte ions to the graphene surface. Therefore, constructing VG arrays in an ordered way is an effective approach to provide rich open channels for the access of electrolytes, enable the shortest straight path for ion exchange, and maximize surface area utilization. As a result, enhance the electrochemical performance of graphene. In 2010, Miller et al. prepared VG arrays on nickel (Ni) substrates using radio frequency plasma enhanced CVD approach [35]. First, the substrates were plasma etched using the mixture of hydrogen and argon. Afterward, methane was used as the carbon source at a total pressure of 85 mT for VG nanosheets arrays growth. The resulting VG nanosheets having a height of 600 nm and <1 nm in thickness. The prepared VG nanosheets were used as electrodes for supercapacitors and finally implemented for the ac line-filtering application. Further, Ren et al. has grown VG inside Ni foam by microwave plasma chemical vapor deposition for supercapacitor application, which can work at a frequency 3–4 orders (in kHz range) higher than traditional EDLCs [89]. Fig. 10(a) shows the digital photograph of bare Ni foam and VG (black color) grown Ni foam, which indicates that VG is grown everywhere on the Ni skeleton. The close view of SEM (cross-sectional) micrograph is provided in Fig. 10(b), which reveals that the graphene sheets were grown in a perpendicular orientation in Ni skeleton. The electrochemical measurement of VG on Ni foam was performed in 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution. Fig. 10(c) represents the CV profile at various scan rates (1 V s−1–500 V s−1). The fabricated supercapacitor device exhibited a specific capacitance of 1.32 mF cm−2 at a scan rate of 1 V s−1 and 0.32 mF cm−2 at a frequency of 1 kHz. In order to fabricate binder free double layer capacitor, Bo et al. prepared VG uniformly grown on a metallic current collector by one-step atmospheric PECVD method [158]. The fabricated binder-free electrode (EDLCs-based) offers the following advantages (i) open channels for better ion access and (ii) exposed edge planes for improved material wettability; compared to other EDLCs. These unique features of binder-free VG result in excellent electrochemical performance in both aqueous and organic electrolytes. Moreover, Seo et al. reported a simple, fast (<10 min), single-step and cost-efficient approach to fabricate vertical oriented graphene arrays on the porous substrate (Ni foam) using natural butter as the precursor without using any hazardous hydrocarbon gases [159]. Fig. 10(d) shows the natural butter was pasted on the Ni foam, and it was converted into VG sheets under the plasma treatment. The SEM micrograph of VG sheets grown on Ni foam is provided in Fig. 10(e), showing the high density of edge planes. The electrochemical characterization of VG sheets was tested in 0.1 M Na2SO4. The CV profile is shown in Fig. 10(f) exhibited a specific capacitance of 230 F g−1 at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Moreover, VG has good electrical conductivity, which can be used as the bridging layer between other electroactive materials and the current collector. For instance, Bo et al. prepared a VG array on nickel foam using an atmospheric dc normal glow discharge PECVD system [160]. The digital image of bare Ni foam and VG sheets grown on Ni foam is shown in Fig. 10(g). The high magnified cross-sectional SEM micrograph of VG array on nickel foam depicted in Fig. 10(h) observes the presence of wrinkles and ripples with open structures, which is beneficial for the electrochemical performance of the open inter-sheet channels. The CV profile of VG array coated Ni foam current collector was recorded at various scan rates and represented in Fig. 10(i), showing nearly rectangular shape at high scan rates (1000 mV s−1).

Few chemical synthesis methods have reported fabricating VG sheets with high mass loading for supercapacitor application. However, these chemical-based synthesis are expensive, complicated, and energy-, time- and resource-demanding. Therefore, Seo et al. developed a simple, low-cost and green approach to synthesize high-quality VG sheets for supercapacitor application [161]. They demonstrated a single-step, plasma-based reforming process to produce VG sheets from natural cheese precursors (as shown in Fig. 11(a)). The VG sheets were grown from cheese on Ni foam substrate by a 10 min exposure to plasmas in an argon and hydrogen gas mixture. Fig. 11(b) represents the CV profile (at scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1) of VG sheets derived from processed cheese in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte, which indicates that the CV shapes were quasi rectangular, reflecting the formation of EDLCs. The VG sheets derived from processed cheese obtained the highest specific capacitance of 153 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.

Generally, VG sheets are hydrophobic in nature, affecting the electrochemical performance of VG sheets due to the incomplete wetting of the electrode. Therefore Ghosh et al. prepared functionalized VG sheets on carbon paper by the in-situ oxygen plasma process [162]. Fig. 11(c) represents the SEM micrograph of activated VG sheets on carbon paper, which indicates the uniform growth and open porous network of activated VG sheets. The electrochemical performance of activated VG sheets was performed in three-electrode system in 1 M TEABF4/H2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 11(d) represents the CV profile of activated VG sheets electrode in hybrid electrolyte, which shows the quasi rectangular-like curve and enhancement in capacitance. The increment in capacitance is attributed to the presence of both cations (TEA+ and H+) and improved wettability in the hybrid electrolyte as shown in the schematic diagram (Fig. 11(e)). The practical application of supercapacitors can be used as micro electronic devices as micro power source (such as microsupercapacitors). Therefore, Aradilla et al. reported the synthesis of VG sheets by electron cyclotron resonance-plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition (ECR-PECVD) method on silicon substrate for microsupercapacitors application [85]. Fig. 11(f) shows the synthesis process of VG sheets by ECR-PECVD method. The high-resolution cross-sectional SEM micrograph of VG sheets is provided in Fig. 11(g), which reflects that the 2 μm thickness of the coating on Si substrate. The synthesized VG sheets were utilized in a symmetric micro-supercapacitor using an aprotic ionic liquid (N-methyl-N-propylpyrrolidinium bis (trifluoromethylsulfonylimide; PYR13TFSI) as electrolyte. The CV profile of fabricated micro-supercapacitors at various scan rates is shown in Fig. 11(h) and it reveals that CV profiles exhibit an excellent capacitive behavior with quasi-rectangular loops even at very high scan rates.

Similarly, Xiong et al. designed a microsupercapacitor using graphitic petals as electrode material [163]. The graphitic petals electrodes were fabricated by using microwave plasma CVD and patterned by optical lithography and reactive ion etching on insulating quartz substrates and Ti/Au current collectors (shown in Fig. 12(a)) and demonstrates that the sharp edges of graphitic petals have better charge storage phenomenon as compared to those of structures that rely on basal plane surfaces. Fig. 12(b) shows the digital photograph of microsupercapacitor. The top view of SEM micrograph of graphitic petals micropatterns is shown in Fig. 12(c), which indicates that the width of the electrodes and the gap between them are approximately 30 and 35 mm, respectively. The electrochemical characterization of fabricated microsupercapacitors was performed after electrochemical oxidization treatment in 1 M H2SO4 solution and the CV profile of microsupercapacitors at various scan rates is shown in Fig. 12(d). The graphitic petals micropatterns-based microsupercapacitor exhibit a maximum energy density of 9.5 mWh cm−3. In order to carry out the in-depth analysis of interfacial charge-transport behaviors and the thermal profiles of VG, Bo et al. performed both experimental and theoretical analysis [164]. Fig. 12(e) shows the schematic diagram for graphene paper-VG-current collector (Ni), which demonstrates that VG is used as the electronic bridges connecting the graphene paper and the current collector. To clarify the fundamental properties of interfaces for both graphene paper-Ni and graphene paper-VG-Ni, DFT simulation has been carried out and shown in Fig. 12(f) and (g). From the DFT simulation, it is calculated that the average separation distance of the graphene paper-Ni interface was 3.21 Å. Similarly, graphene paper-VG-Ni's average separation distances of the graphene paper-VG and VG-Ni interfaces were calculated as 1.40 and 1.37 Å, respectively.

Flexible supercapacitors are attractive for powering the next generation wearable electronic devices. Based upon that binder-free VG-based flexible supercapacitor devices have a great potential for fabrication of flexible and wearable electronic devices [165–167]. For example, Ma et al. designed a ‘tree-like’ morphology of VG using PECVD method for flexible electric double-layer capacitors [168]. The fabricated flexible delivered the highest areal specific capacitance of about 2.45 mF cm−2 with extraordinary flexibility (100,000 times of bending or 180° folding without any loss of capacity). Later on, Sahoo et al. developed a binder-free and current collector-free flexible supercapacitor device using VG electrodes [169]. The fabricated electrode having a very low sheet resistance of 670 Ω cm−2 and provide an excellent symmetric supercapacitor performance (specific capacitance of 158 μF cm−2 with 86 % retention after 10,000 cycles). The fabricated VG based symmetric supercapacitor demonstrates their versatility for flexible and foldable energy storage devices. Recently, Cherusseri and their co-workers reported a novel flexible VG-carbon fiber nanostructure-based supercapacitor device using PVA/H3PO4 gel electrolyte [170]. The fabricated asymmetric supercapacitor device exhibits an excellent gravimetric specific energy of 76 Wh kg−1 with a 100 % capacitance retention even after 1000 bending cycles and used as a flexible and lightweight power source to drive the wearable electronics.

In the view of real time application of supercapacitor devices, AC line filtering is a promising application of supercapacitor. For example, Xu et al. developed electric field assisted PECVD approach to grow VG as electrode for supercapacitor and further perform the AC line filtering application of as prepared supercapacitor device [171]. The prepared VG arrays were grown vertically and bond to each other at a height of ~30 μm, establishing a conductive network in the horizontal direction and micro-channel in the vertical direction. The fabricated VG obtained an outstanding specific areal capacitance of 1.72 mF cm−2 at Φ120 = 80.6° even after 500,000 cycles and well smooth AC waveforms into DC signals, demonstrating excellent filtering performance. Similarly, Bo et al. reported the synthesis of vertically oriented carbon nanowall foam supercapacitor using ionic liquids as electrolyte for high-performance AC line filtering [172]. The fabricated vertically oriented carbon nanowall based supercapacitor device was working in the potential window of 4 V with ultrahigh areal energy density of 1.23 μWh cm−2 at 120 Hz.

The hybridization process can improve the electrochemical performance of VG sheets by integrating pseudocapacitive electrode material (such as transition metal oxides, chalcogenides and polyaniline (PANI)) with VG sheets. Generally, the pseudocapacitive electrodes possess faradic redox reactions for charge storage and they have higher capacitance but lower power density and weaker cyclic stability than EDLCs. On the other hand, VG sheets have high surface area, outstanding mechanical strength, and high conductivity; therefore, they can be used as a scaffold for loading pseudocapacitive nanomaterials with high mass loading. For instance, Xiong et al. demonstrated the growth of hybrid manganese dioxide/graphitic petal structures grown on flexible commercial bucky paper [94].

At first graphitic petals are grown on bucky paper by microwave plasma chemical vapor deposition, and then MnO2 nanoparticles are coated on graphitic petals. The SEM micrograph of MnO2 nanoparticles coated on graphitic petals is shown in Fig. 13(a), which showing a thin uniform layer of MnO2 coated on the smooth graphitic petals surfaces. The electrochemical performance of MnO2 nanoparticles coated on graphitic petals electrode was tested using 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte in three-electrode system. Fig. 13(b) shows the charge-discharge profile of MnO2 nanoparticles coated on graphitic petals electrode, and it displays a symmetric shape of the curve, indicating that the structure has a good electrochemical capacitive behavior. The MnO2 nanoparticles coated on graphitic petals electrode obtained a specific capacitance of 493 F g−1 at a current density of 5 A g−1. Further, to elucidate the fundamental properties at the MnO2/graphitic petals, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out. The schematic diagram of MnO2 clusters and graphene (top view) is shown in Fig. 13(c), in which one layer of graphene and a simple MnO2 cluster were considered.

Similarly, Wang et al. reported the growth of NiO nanoflakes on 3D graphene scaffolds for pseudo-supercapacitor application [173]. At first, 3D graphene scaffold grew on Ni foam using microwave plasma chemical vapor deposition (MPCVD) system under CH4/H2 plasmas by changing the color of Ni foam from shiny white to light black. Then NiO nanoflakes were grown on the surface of the 3D graphene by hydrothermal method. The fabricated NiO-3D graphene hybrid electrode obtained a highest specific capacitance of 1829 F g−1 at a current density of 3 A g−1 in 1 M NaOH electrolyte. Further to evaluate the supercapacitor device performance, an asymmetric device was fabricated using NiO-3D graphene as the positive electrode and activated carbon as the negative electrode. The asymmetric device was operated in the potential window of 1.5 V with the highest device specific capacitance of 440 F g−1 at a current density of 7 A g−1. All these VG sheets are grown on various substrates such as Ni foam and carbon cloth, however, these substrates are generally bulky and limited in surface area, which can hamper the electrochemical performance of graphene.

In order to overcome this issue, VG sheets (also known as graphitic petals) can be used as the substrate to grow other electro-active materials due to their high surface area and electrical conductivity. For instance, Xiong et al. fabricated NiCo hydroxide nanopetals grown on thin free-standing graphene petal foam by two step method [174]. First, the graphene petal foam was grown on Ni foam by MPCVD, followed by chemical etching of the Ni ligaments. Then, NiCo hydroxide nanopetals were electrodeposited on free-standing graphene petal foam. The fabricated electro-active material used as the electrode for supercapacitors and it obtained an areal specific capacitance of 15.3 F cm−2. Further fabricated electrode utilized as the positive electrode for fabrication of asymmetric supercapacitor device and the device exhibited a maximum average energy density of ≈10 mWh cm−3 and maximum power density of ≈3 W cm−3. To fabricate all solid-state supercapacitor, Liao et al. synthesized the hybrid supercapacitor electrode of Co3O4 nanoparticles on VG nanosheets supported by carbon fabric by two-step approach (shown in Fig. 13(d)) [175]. First, VG nanosheets grown on carbon fabric using microwave plasma enhanced CVD system. Later on, the Co3O4 nanoparticles were deposited onto the as prepared VG nanosheets using hydrothermal method. The high-resolution SEM micrograph of hybrid Co3O4-VG nanosheets (shown in Fig. 13(e)) indicates that each sheet's thickness has increased to ~50 nm at hybrid Co3O4-VG nanosheets compared to bare VG, which is proof for the deposition of Co3O4. In the view of practical application, a solid-state flexible symmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated using two hybrid Co3O4-VG electrodes as depicted in Fig. 13(f) and the fabricated symmetric supercapacitor device bent without sacrificing the electrochemical performance. Later on, the same group fabricated a symmetric supercapacitor using Mn3O4 nanoparticles on VG nanosheets supported by carbon fabric, in which VG nanosheets/carbon fabric served as the backbone and current collector for the growth of Mn3O4 [176]. The symmetric supercapacitor device delivered a highest specific capacitance of 50 Wh kg−1 and power density of 64 kW kg−1 with excellent cyclic stability.

Conducting polymers are pseudocapacitive materials that have emerged recently to design advanced energy storage devices. Towards that, the synergistic effect of the VG and conducting polymers for supercapacitor applications are recently studied by some research groups. Towards that, the synergistic effect of the VG and conducting polymers for supercapacitor applications are recently studied by some research groups. Aradilla et al. electrochemically deposited π-conjugated heterocyclic polymers PEDOT (poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)) on vertically-oriented 3D nanoarchitectures, which shows an areal capacitance of 6 mF cm−2. The fabricated micro supercapacitor shows areal capacitance of 2 mF cm−2 and a high volumetric power density of 250 W cm−3 with an outstanding cycling stability 80 % after 300,000 GCD cycles at a current density of 4 mA cm−2 [178]. Further recently Zhou et al. prepared a moldable supercapacitor using a solid-state electrolyte (carboxymethylcellulose and sodium sulphate) and hybrid electrode of VG/MnO2. This foldable supercapacitor achieves a high areal capacitance up to 56 mF cm−2, areal energy density of 7.7 mWh cm−2, and areal power density of 5 mWh cm−2. The porous VG allowing high mass loading deposition of MnO2 and the polymer conductive electrolyte network for efficient electron/ion transport allow the exceptional performance [179]. The same way Sha et al. prepared VG/MnO2 hybrid electrode and used PEGDGE (poly (ethylene glycol) diglycidyl ether) based polymer electrolyte, which exhibited an areal capacitance of 30.7 mF cm−2, energy density of 12.2 mWh kg−1, and power density of 2210.3 mW kg−1 [180]. Zhang et al. deposited ambipolar conducting polymers (2,6-bis(3-methyl-9H-carbazol-9-yl) anthracene-9,10-dione) by multi-scan cyclic voltammetry electropolymerization on VG to improve conductivity and suppressed charge trap effect. The composite electrode material exhibited cycling life (88 % after 10,000 cycles) with high specific area energy density of 19.6 μWh cm−2 (at the power density of 150 μW cm−2) [181]. Apart from this, Zhan et al. fabricated free-standing VG/hydrogen molybdenum bronze/PEDOT composite arrays for sodium-ion battery anode material, which showed high sodium storage capacity of 385 mAh g−1 at 200 mA g−1 with superior cycle stability [182]. Conducting polymers such as PANI have been widely studied as electrode material for supercapacitors due to their good flexible nature. For example, Xiong et al. designed a solid-state flexible supercapacitor using nanometer-thick PANI film conformally coated on graphitic petals grown on conductive carbon cloth by MPCVD and electrochemical polymerization process [96]. The fabricated solid-state flexible supercapacitor device delivered an energy density of 110 Wh kg−1 and a maximum power density of 265 kW kg−1 with excellent cyclic stability. Later on, Tian et al. synthesized vertically aligned holey graphene/PANI heterostructure for electrochemical energy storage application by fabricating a planar micro-supercapacitor [177]. The PANI layer was deposited on VG using electropolymerization process in a three-electrode system. Fig. 13(g) shows the digital image and schematic diagram for vertically aligned holey graphene/PANI heterostructure-based micro-supercapacitors, which displays that the width of each interdigital finger is ~150 μm, and the interspace is ~50 μm. The high-resolution SEM micrograph (as shown in Fig. 13(h)) for vertically aligned holey graphene/PANI heterostructure clearly shows more in-plane space of the graphene intermediate layer. The electrochemical performance of fabricated in plane microsupercapacitor was carried out using PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte. Fig. 13(i) represents the CV profile of in plane microsupercapacitor, which was recorded at various scan rates from 20 to 200 mV s−1. The microsupercapacitor achieved a specific capacitance of 271.1 F cm−3 with energy density of 24.1 mWh cm−3, which can be attributed to the high ionic accessibility during the charge-discharge processes.

In order to obtain better cyclic performance of hybrid electrodes, Seo et al. hybridized VG and CNT electrodes for high performance supercapacitor application [183]. The CNT and VG hybrid structure formed by the direct growth of CNTs onto VG nanosheets by a green plasma-assisted method using natural precursor as butter. The CNT and VG-based hybrid electrode exhibit excellent cyclic stability with retention of >99 % of their initial capacitance after 1000 cycles and a specific capacitance of 278 F g−1. Lin et al. fabricated a unique and novel architecture-based electrode material (vertically standing nanosized graphene and NiO hybrid) for asymmetric supercapacitor devices [184]. Fig. 14(a) represents the schematic diagram for the synthesis of vertical-standing nanosized NiO encapsulated by VG layers directly on conductive substrates using a PECVD process. The VG and NiO hybrid nanosheet arrays have the high surface area and short ion diffusion path, which leads to fast electrochemical reactions with high specific capacitance. The VG and NiO hybrid nanosheet arrays obtain a high specific capacity of 1073 C g−1. Further, an asymmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated using VG-NiO hybrid nanosheet arrays and nitrogen-doped graphene hydrogels as positive and negative electrodes. Fig. 14(b) shows the CV profiles of asymmetric devices that can work up to 1.7 V with a high energy density of 52.6 Wh kg−1. Later on, Ghosh et al. designed a high voltage aqueous asymmetric device MnO2/VG nanosheets and Fe2O3/VG nanosheets as positive and negative electrodes [185]. Fig. 14(c) represents the fabrication process for synthesis of binder-free MnO2/VG nanosheets and Fe2O3/VG nanosheets using PECVD and hydrothermal method. This hybrid structure is beneficial for efficient ion-transport and effective accessibility for the full interior surface, enhancing the supercapacitive performance. The electrochemical performance of as prepared asymmetric supercapacitor device was carried out using 1 M sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) as electrolyte which can operate in the potential window of 2.6 V. The CV profile of asymmetric supercapacitor device is shown in Fig. 14(d) and it obtains a specific capacitance of 76 mF cm−2 at a current density of 2.5 mA cm−2. To achieve high performance flexible supercapacitors for wearable electronics application, Zhou et al. designed a foldable supercapacitor by hybridizing VG nanosheets and MnO2 [179].

The schematic diagram for the fabrication process of foldable wire-shaped supercapacitors is shown in Fig. 14(e). First, VG nanosheets grown on Ni wire using PECVD method and then MnO2 were deposited onto the VG-grafted Ni wire using electrodeposition method. The hybrid VG and MnO2 electrode exhibits a high areal specific capacitance of 56 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. To establish the foldability and flexibility of this hybrid wire-shaped supercapacitors, they were molded into different shapes (as shown in Fig. 14(f)), showing that after folding and molded the supercapacitor device without obvious performance impairment. Transition metal nitrides are also one kind of pseudocapacitive material used for supercapacitor application. Qi et al. reported nanohybrid TiN/VG for high performance supercapacitor application, which achieved an areal capacitance of 9.0 mF cm−2 in the potential window of 1.8 V [186]. A comparison table for the electrochemical performance of VG and their hybrid materials are provided in Table 2 . Three-dimensional assemblies of VG (on various substrates) have been considered as promising strategy to fabricate high performance energy storage devices due to their low density, high conductivity, high surface area and excellent electrochemical stability [187–189]. For example, Hussain et al. reported the growth of three-dimensional Si-VG hybrid for high performance supercapacitor application [190]. At first VG nanosheets were grown on flexible graphite sheet substrate and then silicon was sputtered on the surface of VG to fabricate a supercapacitor electrode. The three-dimensional Si-VG hybrid electrodes were utilized as electrodes for fabrication of a symmetric supercapacitor device using ionic liquid. The symmetric supercapacitor device based on three-dimensional Si-VG hybrid electrodes exhibits highest areal specific capacitance of 16.4 mF cm−2 with excellent cyclic stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tuning the supercapacitor performance by different approaches for the enhanced performance
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical performance of VG nanosheets mainly depends on the various intrinsic activity such as surface area, intrinsic surface reactivity, and electrochemical active sites. These intrinsic properties can be tuned by various modification strategies as shown in Fig. 15 . In the supercapacitor field, the morphology of electroactive material plays a vital role in charge transfer. Graphene and its hybrid electro-active material are widely investigated as the electrode for supercapacitors. However, the serous aggregation and disarrayed alignment of graphene sheets affect the maximum usage of its surface area, which leads to decay in electrochemical performance. Aiming to overcome these issues, the effect of morphology and orientation of vertical graphene sheets plays a crucial role in enhancing the electrochemical performance of graphene-based supercapacitors. For instance, Zhang et al. detail studied the effect of morphology on VG-MnO2 hybrid-based electrodes for supercapacitors application in both experimental and theoretical view [84]. First, VG sheets were grown on graphite paper substrate using MPCVD, then MnO2 was deposited on VG sheets using the chemical deposition method. Fig. 16(a) represents the SEM micrograph for VG sheets coated with MnO2, revealing that MnO2 layer adhered conformally on the VG sheets. Similarly, Fig. 16(b) shows the schematic diagram for the electrolyte ion diffusion direction based on two morphologies of graphene electrodes. The VG sheets have offered an open exchange channel for the electrolyte ion exchange in the shortest straight path. Besides, the ions have to follow a more complicated path to access the bottom in the lateral graphene structure. So that, the VG sheets aid the ion direct transport and exchange process and minimize the ion kinetic losses, which results in better (38 % higher) electrochemical performance than lateral graphene structure.

Vertically graphene arrays are favorable for consistent modification treatment because of their unique structure dispersion and accessibility. However, the fabrication cost of VG graphene is very high. Therefore, Jang et al. followed a novel method for depositing VG structured films onto a carbon fiber paper by drop-casting and evaporating liquid slurry of molecular ligand composited rGO under an electrical field for supercapacitor application [194]. In this novel approach, the molecular ligands aid as “spacer” to reserve interspace in restacked rGO sheets after the film deposits are dried up, which is helpful for enhancing the interspacing of graphene surface accessibility of electrolytes. Similarly, the applied electric field widens interspacing relative to baseline rGO films compared to conventional synthesis of rGO. These structural and morphological modifications are at both nanoscopic and microscopic scales, have led to higher electrochemical performance (such as specific capacitance, energy density and power density) compared to horizontally restacked graphene film electrodes without applied of electric field.

Doping is another key modification approach to enhance the electrochemical performance of VG-based supercapacitors. For instance, Yen et al. reported the direct growth of VG nanowalls via microwave plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition approach on flexible carbon cloths. Later on, nitrogen doping was carried out by in situ NH3 plasma process to form N-doped VG nanowalls. The N-doped VG nanowalls show better electrochemical performance than pristine VG due to the synergistic effect of both electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and the pseudocapacitance, related to the heteroatom function of the materials. The N-doped VG nanowalls obtain the highest specific capacitance of 991.6 F g−1 with an energy density of 275.4 Wh kg−1.

The capacitance of VG sheets is strongly related to the ion-accessible surface area, due to the poor wettability with electrolytes ions they suffer from decay in capacitance. In order to overcome this issue, it is necessary to modify the wettability of VG and enhance the effective surface area. Therefore, Lin et al. developed an effective and novel strategy, based on rotary plasma etching, to simultaneously produce defects on the side surfaces while maintaining the structural integrity of VG sheets (as shown in Fig. 16(c)) [195]. First, VG sheets were grown on Pt/SiO2 substrates by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition. Afterward, the as-grown VG sheets were subjected to Ar-based rotary plasma approaching by rotating the base substrate to induce surface defects on the VG sheets intentionally. Further, the samples were etched at an optimized etching angle of 15° various time periods. The fabricated electrodes are used as the electrodes for supercapacitor application in three electrode system. The etched VG electrode exhibits the highest specific capacitance of 589 μF cm−2 at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1, which could be attributed to the improved wettability of the VG sheets. Fig. 16(d) represents the cyclic stability performance of VG electrode at a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2, which indicates that the VG electrode retained >90 % of its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles. In the view of the practical application of VG-based electrodes suffers from the limited height, which is mostly in a scale of few hundreds of nanometers. So that Han et al. reported an oxygen-assisted “trimming” process to remove the overfrondent graphene nanosheets, thereby overcoming the saturation of the VG sheets thickness during growth [196]. Fig. 16(e) shows the schematic diagram for the fabrication of VG and ultrahigh vertical graphene by using oxygen assisted trimming method, which corrects the morphology of the VG nanosheets and eliminates the thickness saturation of the VG films. Further, the electrochemical characterization of VG and ultrahigh vertical graphene was carried out in three electrode system using 1 M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. The ultrahigh vertical graphene exhibits better (high areal capacitance of 241.35 mF cm−2 at a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2) electrochemical performance compared to VG, which could be attributed to the well-balanced meso-/macroporous structure of the ultrahigh vertical graphene. Recently, Polaki et al. demonstrated the edge-terminations and defect-density of VG by post-deposition plasma treatment under different gas environments (H2, N2 and O2) [53]. The modification enhances the wetting nature of VG in a controlled manner, without destroying their open porous networked structure. The engineered VG electrode shows better electrochemical performance than conventional VG-based electrodes due to the above-mentioned advantages.

In this review, recent developments and advantages on vertical-standing graphene synthesized by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition approach are thoroughly discussed. Vertical graphene arrays have excellent electrochemical performance, especially for supercapacitor applications, so further the recent results and achievements in bare and hybrid VG structures for supercapacitors are summarised.

In spite of these substantial improvements, many challenges remain for the further development (as shown in Fig. 17 ) of VG arrays for supercapacitor applications;
(1)
Although numerous approaches have been developed to fabricate VG-based electrodes, most of these approaches still have shortcomings. It is still demanding to scale up the electrode assembly since the majority of these methods need complex setups and delicate manipulation. Moreover, it is difficult to assemble electrodes with thickness higher than critical cracking thickness because of the intrinsic limitation of slurry casting strategy. Consequently, more economical, sustainable, and facile routes are needed for the fabrication of VG.
(2)
There are various methods have been established, additional developments are still needed for real time applications of VG. There are two vital concerns to be tackled: mechanical strength and electrode density. In case of various vapor deposition method, the intrinsic drawback is that the growth of VG is on the strength of the substrate, thus it is challenging to deposit and/or grow VG directly on various substrates with high thickness. To overcome this issue, electrodes based on VG with high mechanical strength could be fabricated by wood templating and 3D printing methods.
(3)
Even though the vertical graphene growth and mechanism is reported by many groups, still the main challenge is the large-area growth, controlled growth and low temperature growth for different applications like field emissions, sensors, supercapacitor and battery.
(4)
The main aim of supercapacitors is to enhance the energy density of supercapacitor devices by enhancing their potential window. Since VG is proven to be a promising electro-active material for supercapacitors, our main challenge is to enhance the energy density of these VG-based supercapacitors by using organic, ionic or hybrid electrolytes.
(5)
Vertically graphene sheets arrays are usually grown on various conductive substrates such as carbon cloth, Ni foam or metal foam. Consequently, VG sheets' electrochemical performance is affected by many parameters such as the thickness, roughness, and/or mass of the conductive substrates. The hydrophobicity nature of VG sheets exhibit very poor wettability, hampers the access of electrolyte ions on the electroactive surface area of VG, which further chemical treatments and defect engineering can improve.
(6)
In the view of practical application of VG-based supercapacitors, it could be used as micro-electronic devices and/or fabricating micro-supercapacitor devices to power up portable and wearable devices. The investigations of an intrinsic electrochemical mechanism for VG-based microscale devices are still not sufficient. Therefore, VG and its hybrid materials are needed to fabricate micro-supercapacitor with high specific capacitance and energy density. Additionally, flexible VG can be used as the efficient anode material for high-performance flexible Zn-Ion battery as VG are proposed to enable uniform Zn nucleation, thereby obtaining a dendrite-free and robust Zn anode [197].
(7)
Although the VG is emerged as a promising substrate candidate for pseudocapacitive materials, similarly other materials like 3D MXene aerogel and 3D graphene aerogel also evolved. The high surface functional groups help for high mass loading of pseudocapacitive materials. Kong et al. engineered a 3D porous Ti3C2Tx films by reduced-repulsion freeze-casting assembly concept, where the interlayer repulsion is minimized via less electronegative functional groups and charge screening effect based on quantum calculations. The 3D Ti3C2Tx films deliver a capacitance of 207.9 F g−1 with 58.6 % capacitance retention. The high mass loading up to 16.18 mg cm−2, exhibiting ultrahigh areal capacitance of 3731 mF cm−2 and energy density of 336.7 μWh cm−2 [198]. Further Bo et al. also homogeneously loads in-situ-formed, covalently-bonded MnO2 nanosheets on multifunctional MXene. The gravimetric capacitance of MnO2 is enhanced, yielding superior pseudocapacitance of 400 F g−1 at 5 mg cm−2 which is achieved at 10 times lower loadings [199]. As, VG is a pure carbon material, which is helpful for good cyclability and rate capability, layered structure of VG with these 3D structured materials can be engineered for better performance of VG based electrode.

Even though certain challenges persist, the VG-based electrode is still favorable for the electrochemical supercapacitor devices, which require high electrochemical performance such as specific capacitance, specific energy, and specific power. In conclusion, there is still scope for improving the VG-based electrodes' functionalities by overcoming these drawbacks with multidisciplinary and interdisciplinary research from research scientists.
